text: Some transphyncteric fistulas can be very difficult to access with a Lockhart mammary probe and to place a seat on through them. In particular, transphyncteric fistulas which turn through two right angles can be very difficult to access. This is particularly likely to occur where the fistula travels through the ischoanal fossa and then follows a horseshoe path either in the extrasphyncteric or intersphyncteric planes round towards the back of the anus before turning again through another ninety degrees forwards to enter into the anus at the internal opening. This diagram demonstrates the transsphincteric fistula passing through the ischoanal fossa and then turning into an intersphincteric horseshoe. At this point it turns ninety degrees posteriorly to run in the intersphincteric space. When it reaches the six o'clock position it then turns through a second ninety degree angle forwards to pass through the internal sphincter and then open into the anal canal.

The first picture on the left demonstrates the first ninety degree turn into the horseshoe. The second picture on the right shows the second ninety degree turn from the horseshoe track forwards towards the internal opening. Placing a seat on can be very challenging in this situation and the traditional approach is to perform an incision around the circumference of the anus outside the external sphincter to the depth of the tract where it passes into the horseshoe component or through the external sphincter. This straightens out the first of the two right angles and allows the passage of a probe through the second and into the internal opening. The ceton is then threaded through the eye of the probe and brought out through the opening which is then closed. The vaft scope gives us an opportunity to perform this procedure without the need for an incision. We can pass the vaft probe through the external opening and through that first right angle into the horseshoe component.

The surgeon uses a vaft scope to identify the probe at the second right angle of the fistula. The seaton, visible through the scope, is grasped with surgical graspers passed through the instrument port. The seaton is then pulled out of the fistula as the vaft scope and graspers are withdrawn, allowing it to be tied from the internal opening to the external opening.

The surgeon ties a ceton using one ethibond with a single surgeon's knot, followed by tying the whiskers back with a further single surgeon's knot using two-oh silk. This technique results in a low-profile, soft ceton that is visually non-bulky and smooth. The ceton is placed through complex fistula tracts using a minimally invasive approach, without requiring an incision, even when the tract has multiple right angles.
